did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used AI/ML methods including Canny and Sobel edge detection for segmentation, Convolutional Neural Networks (ResNet-50, EfficientNet, VGGNet) for deep feature extraction, and Support Vector Machine (SVM) with Radial Basis Function for classification to detect and classify skin lesions (melanoma, nevus, seborrheic keratosis) from the ISIC dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents an original experimental study proposing a new framework called 'EdgeCanSob' for automated skin lesion detection. It details specific methods including Canny and Sobel edge detection, CNNs (ResNet, EfficientNet, VGGNet), and SVM classification. It reports original results, performance metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity), and statistical analysis (t-tests, p-values) on a dataset, which aligns with the definition of a research paper producing new results. Although published in a conference proceedings (ICOCT), it contains original experimental findings rather than being a review or survey.

title: Automated Skin Lesion Detection Using Edge Detection and Deep Feature Analysis
brief_summary: This study proposes the EdgeCanSob framework for automated skin lesion detection and classification using the ISIC dataset. The method integrates Canny and Sobel edge detection for segmentation with deep feature extraction via Convolutional Neural Networks (ResNet-50, EfficientNet, VGGNet) and classification using a Support Vector Machine with a Radial Basis Function. The evaluation approach involved assessing performance metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity, alongside statistical analysis using independent sample t-tests. The results demonstrated that the EdgeCanSob framework achieved a mean accuracy of 94.07% and a peak accuracy of 98.85%, significantly outperforming the VGGNet model which achieved 91.12% accuracy. The study concludes that the hybrid approach of edge detection and deep feature analysis offers a scalable and cost-effective solution for early melanoma prediction.

lesion_types_studied.details: Identified as a major cause of mortality worldwide and a key class in the ISIC dataset used for the study. The paper focuses on early prediction of melanoma.

The ISIC dataset used in the study is described as having labeled benign and malignant skin lesions, with melanoma specified as an example of a malignant class. The paper also refers generally to 'cancer skin lesion'.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion classes (melanoma, nevus, seborrheic keratosis) and benign vs malignant status
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the use of Support Vector Machine (SVM) for 'classification of skin lesion dataset' and 'Melanoma Classification'. It reports classification metrics including accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, recall, and F1-score. The framework EdgeCanSob is designed to classify input images into specific diagnostic categories.

The paper mentions using Canny and Sobel edge detection for 'segmentation' to extract 'raw segmentation mask' and refine it with post-processing. However, this is described as a preprocessing/feature extraction step for the primary classification task, not the final predictive output.

datasets_used.name: ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collaboration) dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing labeled benign and malignant skin lesions, specifically including melanoma, nevus, and seborrheic keratosis. The paper also references a sample dataset structure in Table I with patient history (age, sex, region, shape, area) for 10 records, though the primary analysis uses the larger ISIC collection.

use.training: Used for training the EdgeCanSob framework and comparing with VGGNet, with performance evaluated across varying dataset sizes (from 7985 to 15654 samples).
use.testing: Used for testing and evaluation of accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, recall, and F1-score.
use.other: Used for statistical analysis (t-tests) comparing EdgeCanSob and VGGNet models.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Applied to compare the accuracy outcomes of the proposed EdgeCanSob framework against traditional machine learning algorithm classifiers.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in the accuracy outcomes between the proposed EdgeCanSob framework and traditional machine learning algorithm classifiers.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in the accuracy outcomes between the proposed EdgeCanSob framework and traditional machine learning algorithm classifiers.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Statistical significance was assessed using p-values and confidence intervals (95%) to evaluate the significance of observed associations between the Cancer Skin Lesion dataset and feature extraction/classification. The study also utilized G-power analysis with a power of 80 percent.

ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collabo.description: Dataset containing labeled benign and malignant skin lesions (melanoma, nevus, seborrheic keratosis). Evaluated across varying sample sizes (N=1 to 10) corresponding to dataset sizes from 15,654 down to 7,985.
ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collabo.notes: The paper also reports a peak accuracy of 98.85% for EdgeCanSob and a mean accuracy of 94.07%. In the conclusion, an accuracy of 98.11% is mentioned for the proposed system, though Table III details 98.85% as the peak for N=1.

architecture_used.main_model: EdgeCanSob (Hybrid framework combining Canny and Sobel edge detection with Convolutional Neural Networks and Support Vector Machine)

processing_steps: Pre-processing (contrast enhancement via histogram equalization, noise reduction via Gaussian filtering, normalization)

preprocessing_techniques: Normalization to ensure uniform intensity level across images

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Mentioned as part of the pre-processing steps involving resize, normalize, and augment, but specific techniques, probabilities, or ranges are not reported.

conclusions: The proposed EdgeCanSob framework, which integrates Canny and Sobel edge detection with deep feature extraction using Convolutional Neural Networks (ResNet-50, EfficientNet) and classification via Support Vector Machine (SVM), achieves superior performance compared to traditional machine learning models and CNN-based models like VGGNet.

EdgeCanSob demonstrated a peak accuracy of 98.85% and a mean accuracy of 94.07% on the ISIC dataset, significantly outperforming the VGGNet model which achieved a peak accuracy of 91.12% and a mean accuracy of 88.76%.
The difference in accuracy between EdgeCanSob and VGGNet was statistically significant (p < 0.05, p-value = 0.000), as determined by independent sample t-tests with a 95% confidence interval.

The performance of the EdgeCanSob model is dependent on dataset size, with accuracy decreasing as the number of training samples decreases (ranging from 98.85% with 15,654 samples to 90.88% with 7,985 samples).

The hybrid approach of edge detection and deep feature analysis offers a scalable, cost-effective, and efficient solution for the early prediction of skin cancer lesions, potentially assisting dermatologists in improving healthcare quality.
The study acknowledges limitations in capturing effective feature extraction for multi-dimensional datasets with high-order parameters using the EdgeCanSob framework.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges a limitation in the EdgeCanSob framework's ability to capture effective feature extraction for multi-dimensional datasets with high-order parameters.
The paper does not report specific data leakage prevention measures or detailed cross-validation strategies beyond evaluating performance across varying dataset sizes (N=1 to 10).
The dataset description in Table I contains implausible entries (e.g., 'Annul Upper ar Back' for Region/Shape), suggesting potential data quality or transcription errors in the sample records.
There is a discrepancy in reported peak accuracy for EdgeCanSob: Table III shows 98.85% for N=1, while the Conclusion states 98.11%.
The paper does not report preprocessing details such as input image resolution, specific augmentation probabilities, or training hyperparameters (optimizer, loss function, learning rate).
The study relies on the ISIC dataset, which may introduce population bias regarding skin tones and geographic demographics, a challenge noted in the related work section.
The statistical analysis uses an independent sample t-test with a very small sample size (N=10) for the comparison, which may limit the robustness of the statistical significance claims (p=0.000).
The paper does not provide external validation results; performance is evaluated solely on the ISIC dataset without testing on independent external cohorts.
The framework's performance is shown to be highly dependent on dataset size, with accuracy dropping from 98.85% (15,654 samples) to 90.88% (7,985 samples).
